title: Large Covered Sandbox
summary: A big 2x8 sandbox with wide sit-on top trim and four canopy posts ready for a sun cover. The posts double as corner reinforcement.
description:
This is a large sandbox — roughly 6-1/2 feet square inside — framed from 2x8 lumber with a wide 1x3 top trim you can sit on, and four 2x2 canopy posts that carry a sun cover overhead. It's a straightforward outdoor build, but it's designed cleverly: several parts do double duty, so you get a stronger box for the same effort.

The two structural ideas worth understanding. First, the top trim overlaps the corner joints of the 2x8 sides, so screwing it into both sides at each corner reinforces those joints — a sandbox full of sand puts real outward pressure on the corners, and this ties them together. Second, the canopy posts aren't just for the cover; they act as corner blocking, screwed to both side pieces with eight screws per corner, which is what makes the corners genuinely strong.

Start with site prep, because a sandbox that isn't level looks and drains wrong. Dig and rake a level pad, build the box, and level it with rocks as shims or by cutting down high spots. Use outdoor-appropriate lumber if it'll sit in the weather for years — the plan uses standard framing lumber, but treated or cedar lasts far longer in ground contact. Add a fabric canopy across the top posts and a bottom liner or hardware cloth to keep the sand in and the critters out.

tools.note: Levelling the box on the ground is half the job.
For attaching a fabric canopy or a bottom liner.
Knock down splinters where kids will sit and reach.

materials.name: 2x8, 8 ft
materials.note: Sandbox sides. Treated or cedar for longevity.

materials.name: Canopy fabric and bottom liner
materials.note: Optional — sun cover and a weed/critter barrier.

steps.title: Prep the site and build the box
steps.body: Dig and rake a level surface for the sandbox first — this is the step people skip and regret. Build the box from the 2x8 sides, fastening the corners with 3" screws; predrill and countersink so the ends don't split. Set it on the pad and level it using rocks as shims or by digging down the high spots.

steps.title: Add the top trim to the long sides
steps.body: Fasten the top trim to the two LONGER sides first with 2" screws, keeping inside edges flush. This wide trim is what kids sit on, and it starts tying the box together.

steps.title: Finish the trim across the corners
steps.body: Add the remaining top trim, screwing it so it overlaps the corner joints of the 2x8 sides. Fasten it into BOTH sides at each corner — that overlap is what reinforces the joint against the outward push of a full sandbox.

steps.title: Install the canopy posts as corner blocking
steps.body: Stand the four 2x2 canopy posts in the corners. They double as corner blocking, so screw each into both side pieces — two screws per side, eight per corner. This is what makes the corners genuinely strong, not just the trim.

steps.title: Frame the canopy top and add the cover
steps.body:
Build the canopy top frame from the 1x4 sides across the tops of the posts, and add the 45°-mitered corner braces to keep it square and straight — check the frame is square before fastening the braces.

Stretch a fabric canopy over the top frame and lay a bottom liner in the box if you want a weed and critter barrier. Sand any splinters where kids sit and reach.

images.alt: Large wooden sandbox with a canopy frame over the top
